_comment: Source: NASEMSO National Model EMS Clinical Guidelines v3.0 (March 2022). Cardiovascular: Bradycardia. NASEMSO national provisional base.
level_display: All Levels — Interventions Scoped Per Provider Level
protocol_reference: NASEMSO National Model EMS Clinical Guidelines v3.0 — Cardiovascular: Bradycardia

sections.title: Patient Care Goals
sections.reference: NASEMSO National Model EMS Clinical Guidelines v3.0 — Cardiovascular: Bradycardia
points: Maintain adequate perfusion.
Treat underlying cause:. a. Hypoxia

sections.title: Patient Presentation
points: Heart rate less than 60 beats per minute (BPM) with either symptoms (altered mental status (AMS), chest pain (CP), congestive heart failure (CHF), seizure, syncope, shock, pallor, diaphoresis) or evidence of hemodynamic instability
The major EKG rhythms classified as bradycardia include:
See additional inclusion criteria for pediatric patients
EXCLUSION CRITERIA:
None noted

sections.title: EMR Interventions
sections.reference: NASEMSO v3.0 — EMR level (Emergency Medical Responder)
points: Oxygen via NRM targeting SpO2 94-98%
Supine position with legs elevated if hypotensive
CPR if patient becomes pulseless; AED if shockable rhythm develops
Identify and treat hypoxia (primary cause of pediatric bradycardia)

sections.title: EMT Interventions
sections.reference: NASEMSO v3.0 — EMT level (Emergency Medical Technician)
points: 12-lead ECG acquisition and transmission
Supraglottic airway if airway compromise
CPAP if pulmonary edema component
IV maintenance (non-medicated)

sections.title: AEMT Interventions
points: IV/IO access initiation
IV/IO fluid bolus 250-500 mL NS for hypotension
Waveform capnography
Atropine 1 mg IV/IO for symptomatic bradycardia (max 3 mg total) — AEMT level per state/local protocol

sections.title: Paramedic Interventions
points: Atropine 1 mg IV/IO q 3-5 min (max 3 mg) for symptomatic sinus bradycardia or AV block
Transcutaneous pacing for high-degree AV block or bradycardia unresponsive to atropine; sedate/analgize before pacing
Epinephrine infusion 0.02-0.2 mcg/kg/min IV or push-dose epinephrine (10-20 mcg boluses) for refractory hypotension
Norepinephrine infusion 0.02-0.4 mcg/kg/min IV for refractory hypotension
Dopamine infusion 2-20 mcg/kg/min as alternative vasopressor
Pediatric: epinephrine 0.01 mg/kg IV/IO for bradycardia with poor perfusion
12-lead ECG interpretation for rhythm identification and management
Consider overdose (beta-blocker, CCB, digoxin) as cause; treat per toxicology protocol

sections.title: Patient Safety Considerations
points: If pacing is performed, consider sedation or pain control

sections.title: Educational Pearls
points: Observe for signs of decreased end-organ perfusion: chest pain (CP), shortness of breath (SOB), decreased level of consciousness, syncope, or other signs of shock/hypotension.
Patients who have undergone cardiac transplant will not respond to atropine.
Consider potential culprit medications including beta-blockers, calcium channel blockers, sodium channel blockers/anti-depressants, digoxin, and clonidine

The differential diagnosis includes the following: myocardial infarction (MI), hypoxia, pacemaker failure, hypothermia, sinus bradycardia, athletes, head injury with increased intracranial pressure (ICP), stroke, spinal cord lesion, sick sinus syndrome, AV blocks, overdose, cholinergic nerve agents
Consider hyperkalemia in the patient with wide complex bradycardia
Bradycardia should be managed via the least invasive manner possible, escalating care as needed
Be aware of acute coronary syndrome as a cause of bradycardia in adult patients
When dosing medications for pediatric patients, dose should be weight-based for non-obese patients and based on ideal body weight for obese patients

sections.title: Key Documentation
points: ' Cardiac rhythm/rate
' Time, dose, and response of medications given
' Pacing: Time started or stopped, rate, joules, capture, and response rate
' Patient weight.
' Pediatric length-based tape color (for pediatrics who fit on tape)
' History of event supporting treatment of underlying causes

EMR: Scene safety, primary survey, CPR, AED, BVM, bleeding control, oral airway, O2 therapy, epinephrine auto-injector for anaphylaxis, naloxone unit-dose auto-injector
EMT: EMR scope PLUS: NPA, supraglottic airway, CPAP, pulse oximetry, 12-lead ECG acquisition, oral glucose, aspirin, patient own nitroglycerin, albuterol SVN, IM epinephrine, blood glucose monitoring, cervical collar, traction splint
AEMT: EMT scope PLUS: waveform capnography, IO access, IV access initiation, IV/IO fluids, IV medications (dextrose, naloxone, epinephrine, glucagon, analgesics, antiemetics), subcutaneous/intranasal medications, nitroglycerin from stock
Paramedic: AEMT scope PLUS: ET intubation, RSI, cricothyrotomy, needle decompression, manual defibrillation, cardioversion, transcutaneous pacing, 12-lead ECG interpretation, all antidysrhythmics, vasopressors, all medication routes and classes

deterioration_flags: Heart rate less than 60 beats per minute (BPM) with either symptoms (altered mental status (AMS), chest pain (CP), congestive heart failure (CHF), seizure, syncope, shock, pallor, diaphoresis) or evidence of hemodynamic instability
scope_notes: EMR scope is the minimum national standard — state/MCA may expand
All interventions subject to local medical director credentialing
Pediatric patients: use weight-based dosing and age-appropriate equipment
Contact medical direction when required by local protocol or for interventions outside standard scope
